planting.note: Nick or soak seeds for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination; direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, or sow in fall for spring germination. Perennial sweet pea grows from a deep, fleshy rootstock and is difficult to transplant — direct seeding or container-starts in deep pots are recommended.

directions: Nick seeds and sow in early spring at the base of a trellis, fence, or shrub; perennial sweet pea is a vigorous, deep-rooted climbing perennial that produces small clusters of magenta-pink, rose, or white flowers from June through August — different in color and somewhat smaller than the annual sweet pea but with the same climbing, clasping, and twining habit. It climbs by tendrils to 6–10 feet and tolerates poor, dry soils and heat far better than the annual sweet pea, making it an easy-care perennial vine for difficult spots. Unlike the annual, it has no fragrance, a tradeoff for its tough perennial character. It self-sows readily and can naturalize on disturbed soil and roadsides. The 'White Pearl' and 'Red Pearl' selections provide specific flower color control. All parts of the plant are mildly toxic if consumed in large quantities.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to a very well-drained site in full sun; sunset hyssop requires excellent drainage and thrives in lean, rocky, alkaline soils — rich, moist soils promote disease and weak growth. Established plants are very drought-tolerant and heat-loving.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; sunset hyssop is a native agastache of the dry mountains of New Mexico and Arizona, producing aromatic, narrow, gray-green leaves with an intensely pleasant combined licorice-mint-root-beer fragrance and long, narrow spikes of small tubular flowers in a spectacular orange-pink to salmon-coral color that shades toward orange at the base of the tube and pink at the tip — precisely the color of a sunset sky. The blooms appear from midsummer through fall and are intensely attractive to hummingbirds throughout the season. Unlike the more common anise hyssop, sunset hyssop is more heat-tolerant, much more drought-tolerant, and thrives in the hot, arid, alkaline soils of the Southwest and Gulf Coast. It is short-lived (2–4 years) but self-sows readily. Deer resistant.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall, or divide established clumps in early spring; eastern beebalm is more drought-tolerant and mildew-resistant than the common scarlet beebalm (Monarda didyma) and is better adapted to dry, thin, alkaline soils. It blooms 2–4 weeks earlier than other beebalm species.

directions: Sow seeds or divide in spring in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; eastern beebalm is the most compact and drought-tolerant of the native monardas, producing a tidy mound of aromatic foliage topped in late spring (May–June) with lavender-white to pale pink flowers with distinctive purple-spotted lips that are excellent for bumblebees, native sweat bees, and early-season butterflies. Unlike the taller, powdery-mildew-prone Monarda didyma, eastern beebalm stays under 2 feet and has outstanding mildew resistance even in humid climates. It blooms substantially earlier than other beebalms, bridging the gap between spring flowers and summer bloomers in the pollinator garden. Native to prairies, open woodlands, and rocky roadsides from Illinois to Kansas and south to the Gulf Coast, it is particularly well adapted to the heat and variable moisture of the South and Midwest.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained soil after last frost; germander sage requires excellent drainage and lean soil — rich, moist soils cause weak, floppy growth and crown rot. It thrives in alkaline, rocky, caliche soils of Texas and the Southwest where it acts as an evergreen perennial to subshrub in zones 7–9.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; germander sage is a small evergreen to semi-evergreen subshrub from the dry mountains of Mexico producing small, rounded, silver-gray to blue-gray leaves on compact, arching stems and brilliant electric-blue flowers from spring through fall — the striking cerulean-blue color of the flowers is more vivid and saturated than almost any other hardy sage. Native to rocky, limestone soils of the Sierra Madre and Chihuahuan Desert foothills at 4,000–8,000 feet elevation, it is adapted to the alkaline, well-drained soils common in Texas, New Mexico, and the Gulf Coast region. It is one of the best low-water-use ornamental plants for Texas gardens: compact, deer-resistant, drought-tolerant, and in near-continuous bloom. Excellent in rock gardens, xeriscapes, and as a low edging plant.

planting.note: Root cuttings in late spring or plant after last frost; Andean sage grows as a tender perennial in zones 9–11 and can be overwintered indoors as a container plant in colder climates. It prefers well-drained, fertile soil and benefits from light fertilization during the growing season.

directions: Root cuttings in warm conditions and transplant to well-drained soil in part shade to full sun; Andean sage is one of the most extraordinary and unusual plants in the genus, producing a stark color contrast that stops visitors in their tracks: the flowers are an unusually deep, near-black navy-indigo color, emerging from large, pale jade-green calyces that make the dark flowers appear to float against a pale background — the visual effect is dramatic and completely unlike any other sage. The sticky, apple-scented foliage has white undersides and slightly viscid texture. Native to the Andean cloud forests of Peru, it blooms from late summer through fall and is reliably perennial in frost-free zones 9–11. The dark flowers and pale calyces are highly attractive to hummingbirds and make an excellent contrast with silver, gold, or pink companion plants.
